title: NOAA quarto simple
text:
ReadMe for Figs Folder
The purpose of this folder is hold all figures generated for the analysis. Your code to create figures should be saved in the code folder, and it should automatically save figure output directly to this figs folder. All figure names should be descriptive not based on the # of the figure (as this may change).
Note: to create a template with standardized folder structure, there needs to be a document within the folder to save to github. This readme serves as this document, and can be deleted from your personal project/repository.

title: Examples Dashboard
Dashboard Setup & Maintenance Guide
text:
This document covers everything you need to set up, populate, maintain, and extend the Examples Dashboard as a template repository owner or contributor.



your-repo/
│
├── dashboard.css                         ← All dashboard styling (root level)
│
├── _code/
│   ├── examples.yaml                     ← Single source of truth for all examples
│   ├── example-submission.yml            ← Reference copy of the issue form template
│   ├── process_submission.yml            ← Reference copy of the GitHub Action workflow
│   └── parse_submission.py               ← Reference copy of the parser script
│
├── content/
│   ├── dashboard.qmd                     ← Main dashboard page
│   ├── instructions.qmd                  ← This guide
│   └── card-images/                      ← Thumbnail/screenshot images
│       ├── placeholder.svg               ← Fallback shown when no image is set
│       └── my-example.png                ← One image file per example
│
└── .github/
    ├── ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
    │   └── example-submission.yml        ← Working copy (GitHub reads this)
    ├── workflows/
    │   └── process_submission.yml        ← Working copy (GitHub reads this)
    └── scripts/
        └── parse_submission.py           ← Called by the Action at runtime

_code/ vs .github/: The files in _code/ are your editable source copies — edit them there, then copy the relevant files to their .github/ locations so GitHub can find them. See the Keeping copies in sync section below.







dashboard.qmd has a PATH CONFIGURATION block near the top of its R chunk. This is the single place to update if you ever reorganise the repository. Before deploying, replace YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO with your actual GitHub organisation and repository name:
# ── PATH CONFIGURATION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
path_data        <- here("_code", "examples.yaml")
path_images      <- "card-images"
path_placeholder <- "card-images/placeholder.svg"
submit_url       <- paste0(
  "https://github.com/YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO",   # ← update this
  "/issues/new?template=example-submission.yml"
)



The dashboard uses the yaml package to read examples.yaml. Install it once:
install.packages("yaml")
Your renv.lock (if you use renv) should capture this automatically when you next run renv::snapshot().



The placeholder image is shown on cards where no image has been provided. A minimal SVG is included at content/card-images/placeholder.svg. You can replace it with your own branded placeholder — any common image format works (SVG, PNG, JPG). If you rename it, update path_placeholder in the PATH CONFIGURATION block of dashboard.qmd.



A minimal Quarto website configuration:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

website:
  title: "Your Project Name"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: content/dashboard.qmd
        text: Examples
      - href: content/instructions.qmd
        text: Guide

format:
  html:
    theme: default



In your repository: Settings → Pages → Source → Deploy from branch → main → /docs.
After this, every push to main that includes a re-rendered docs/ folder will update the live site.







All examples live in _code/examples.yaml. Each entry is one card on the dashboard. The file begins with a detailed comment header — read it before making edits.











Field
Required
Type
Notes




id
✅
integer
Unique. Always increment from the last entry.


title
✅
string
Short title for the card (~60 chars).


description
✅
string
1–3 sentences. Use the > block scalar for long text.


url
✅
string
Full public URL.


tools
✅
list
One or more tool categories (see below).


keywords
✅
list
One or more keywords (see below).


image
—
string
Filename only (e.g. my-example.png). File must be in content/card-images/. Leave blank "" for placeholder.


author
—
string
Person or organisation.


date_added
—
string
ISO date YYYY-MM-DD. For internal tracking.






- id: 7
  title: "My Open Science Workflow"
  description: >
    A reproducible end-to-end workflow for open science data analysis,
    from raw data ingestion through to publication-ready figures.
  url: "https://github.com/example/open-workflow"
  tools:
    - Reproducible Reports
    - Data Management
  keywords:
    - r
    - GitHub
    - template
  image: "open-workflow.png"
  author: "Jane Smith"
  date_added: "2025-03-01"



Unlike CSV, multi-value fields are just YAML lists — no need to wrap comma-separated values in quotes. Each tool or keyword is its own indented - item line.






Open _code/examples.yaml in RStudio or any text editor.
Copy an existing entry block and paste it at the bottom of the examples: list.
Increment id by 1 from the previous last entry.
Fill in the fields.
If you have an image, save it in content/card-images/.
Render (quarto render) and verify the card appears correctly.
Commit and push.





Tools and keywords do not need to be registered anywhere — the dashboard derives its dropdown options automatically from all values present in _code/examples.yaml at render time.
To add a new tool or keyword: use it in a new (or existing) entry. It will appear in the dropdown on the next render.
To remove a tool or keyword from the dropdowns: remove it from every entry that uses it.
To rename a tool or keyword: do a find-and-replace across _code/examples.yaml.
The current default values are listed in the GitHub issue form template (_code/example-submission.yml / .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/example-submission.yml) — update both copies when you add new official tools or keywords so submitters see the full list.







1. Community member opens the GitHub issue using the "Submit a New
   Dashboard Example" template and fills in the structured form.

2. The issue is auto-labelled "example-submission" and "needs-review".

3. Admin reviews the issue content.

4. If approved, admin adds the "approved" label.
       ↓
   GitHub Action fires automatically:
     a. Parses the issue form body
     b. Appends a new entry to _code/examples.yaml
     c. Opens a Pull Request on a branch named example/issue-N

5. Admin reviews the PR:
     a. Checks the new YAML entry in _code/examples.yaml looks correct
     b. Downloads any submitted image → saves to content/card-images/
     c. Verifies any new tool/keyword values are intentional
     d. Runs quarto preview locally (optional but recommended)

6. Admin merges the PR.

7. Admin re-renders and pushes docs/ (or CI does it automatically).

8. New card appears live on the dashboard. ✅




The workflow file (.github/workflows/process-submission.yml) is already in place. It needs two things before it will work:


Go to your repo → Issues → Labels → New label and create:








Label
Colour
Purpose




example-submission
any
Auto-applied by the issue template. Identifies submission issues.


needs-review
yellow
Auto-applied. Signals it’s awaiting admin review.


approved
green
Applied manually by admins to trigger the Action.


auto-generated
grey
Applied to PRs created by the Action.






Go to: Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions Select: Read and write permissions → Save.
This allows the Action to commit to the repository and open PRs using GITHUB_TOKEN (no additional secrets needed).






Open the new issue (labelled example-submission + needs-review).
Review: is the URL valid and publicly accessible? Is the description accurate? Are the tool/keyword selections appropriate?
If you want to reject it, close the issue with a comment explaining why.
If you approve it, add the approved label. The Action will run within ~1 minute.
Check the Actions tab to confirm the workflow completed successfully.
Find the new PR (it’s linked in a comment automatically posted to the issue).
In the PR, review _code/examples.yaml in the diff.
If the submitter provided an image URL:

Download the image.
Rename it to match the image field in the new YAML entry.
Add it to content/card-images/ and commit it to the PR branch.

Merge the PR.
Re-render and push (quarto render → git add docs/ → git push).





If you prefer not to use the Action, or if something fails:

Read the issue carefully and extract the submitted values.
Open _code/examples.yaml and manually append a new entry.
If an image was provided, download it to content/card-images/.
Commit, push, re-render.





The files in _code/ are your source of record — the copies you edit in RStudio alongside your other project files. The .github/ copies are what GitHub actually reads and executes.
When you edit _code/example-submission.yml (e.g. to add new tools or keywords to the form checkboxes), copy it to .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/:
cp _code/example-submission.yml .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/example-submission.yml
When you edit _code/process_submission.yml (e.g. to change Action behaviour), copy it to .github/workflows/:
cp _code/process_submission.yml .github/workflows/process_submission.yml
_code/parse_submission.py is the script actually called by the Action (via .github/scripts/parse_submission.py). Keep these in sync the same way:
cp _code/parse_submission.py .github/scripts/parse_submission.py

Tip: You can automate these copies with a short shell script or a pre-commit hook if you find yourself editing these files frequently.




# In your project directory (or use RStudio's Render button):
quarto render

# Live preview in browser with hot reload:
quarto preview



The standard workflow after any content update:
quarto render          # rebuilds docs/
git add docs/
git commit -m "render: update dashboard"
git push
GitHub Pages will serve the updated docs/ automatically within ~30 seconds.



Add a second workflow to render the site automatically on every push to main:
# .github/workflows/render-site.yml
name: Render and Deploy Site
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r@v2
        with:
          use-public-rspm: true

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies@v2
        with:
          packages: |
            any::tidyverse
            any::here
            any::yaml
            any::jsonlite

      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Render site
        run: quarto render

      - uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@v4
        with:
          folder: docs







All visual tokens are CSS custom properties at the top of dashboard.css (in the root directory). Change these to retheme the entire dashboard:
:root {
  --ink:        #1a1410;   /* primary text */
  --ivory:      #f7f3ec;   /* page background */
  --terra:      #c4583a;   /* tool tag accent */
  --teal:       #2a6b6e;   /* keyword tag accent */
  --serif:      "Fraunces", Georgia, serif;
  --sans:       "DM Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
  --mono:       "DM Mono", monospace;
}
To swap fonts: update the Google Fonts <link> tag in the include-in-header section of content/dashboard.qmd, and update the --serif/--sans/--mono variables in dashboard.css.



By default, selecting multiple tools uses OR logic: a card appears if it matches any selected tool. To require all selected tools to match (AND logic), edit the filtering OJS cell in content/dashboard.qmd:
// Change .some() to .every() for AND logic:
const toolMatch =
  selectedTools.length === 0 ||
  selectedTools.every(t => exTools.includes(t))   // ← every, not some

const kwMatch =
  selectedKeywords.length === 0 ||
  selectedKeywords.every(k => exKws.includes(k))  // ← every, not some




Add the new field to entries in _code/examples.yaml.
In the R chunk in content/dashboard.qmd, add the field to the map_dfr() tibble.
In the OJS cards cell, reference ex.your_field inside renderCard().




When you add new official tools or keywords to _code/examples.yaml, also add them to the checkboxes in _code/example-submission.yml, then copy that file to .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/example-submission.yml so submitters see the full list.






Dashboard shows no examples / blank page

Confirm _code/examples.yaml exists and is valid YAML (no tab characters — use spaces). Run yaml::read_yaml(here("_code", "examples.yaml")) in R to check. Verify the here() path in the PATH CONFIGURATION block resolves correctly from your project root.

Images not appearing on cards

The filename in the image field must exactly match the file in content/card-images/ (case-sensitive on Linux/GitHub). The card silently falls back to the placeholder on load failure — open your browser’s DevTools console to see the actual 404 URL.

New tools/keywords not appearing in dropdowns

The dropdowns are built from the data at render time. Re-render after editing _code/examples.yaml.

GitHub Action not firing on “approved” label

Check the label name is spelled exactly approved (case-sensitive). Check the Actions tab for errors. Confirm workflow permissions are set to “Read and write” in repository Settings.

parse_submission.py producing empty or wrong field values

GitHub renders issue form fields as ### Field Label\n\nvalue. If GitHub changes this format, the regex in the script may need updating. Print ISSUE_BODY in the Action’s logs to inspect the raw text, then adjust the extract_field() patterns in _code/parse_submission.py, and copy the updated file to .github/scripts/parse_submission.py.

_code/ and .github/ copies are out of sync

If the Action is behaving unexpectedly, check whether you edited _code/ but forgot to copy to .github/. See the Keeping copies in sync section.

renv doesn’t include the yaml package

Run install.packages("yaml") then renv::snapshot() to capture it.






If you improve this dashboard template and want to share changes upstream, please open an issue or PR in the original template repository. Suggestions for new default tools/keywords, design improvements, workflow enhancements, and accessibility fixes are all welcome.

Once you have finished updating your workflow so that the forms are all ‘complete’, then you might want to transition this workflow into your lab manual or research compendium and change links or metadata associated with each flow chart component.

title: Making a Decision
text: This section will allow the user to take their survey results and apply them to a function to return some recommendations and considerations for their decisions.

title: NOAA quarto simple
text:
ReadMe for Data Folder
The purpose of this folder is hold ORIGINAL raw or raw-derived data. Github is NOT an appropriate platform for saving large files or large numbers of files. You will need to save your raw data (and possible other large data products) in an alternative location, such as NCEI, Figshare, or Zenodo. We recommend that you identify the location of all raw data (WHERE?). As this data is intended to be ORIGINAL data, we recommend that modified or intermediate data or data products (that are modified by code stored in the code folder) be stored in the output folder.
Note: to create a template with standardized folder structure, there needs to be a document within the folder to save to github. This readme serves as this document, and can be deleted from your personal project/repository.

title: Home
text:
Openscapes Pathways
Openscapes is an open source approach and movement that helps researchers do better science, together. The Openscapes Champions Program is an open data science mentorship program for science teams, and it begins by identifying an individual or teams’ ‘Pathway’. The Pathways concept identifies how work is currently being done, and where they would like to go in the future with respect to reproducibility, collaboration, communication, and culture. This first step traditionally begins with a self-assessment using a spreadsheet template.





Navigating the landscape of our scientific journey; Openscapes encourages identifying common needs and working with teams to navigate the landscape.








Example of spreadsheet approach to identifying the components of your path, from where you are now, to where you want to go.




For those new to open science and with limited exposure to alternative scientific cultures or ways of doing science, it can be difficult to identify what exactly you want and need. By the end of the Openscapes Champions Program, this vision of these alternatives become clear and these initial projects frequently include developing a Lab Manual (a one-stop shop for everything) or automation of part of their workflow to improve reproducibility. T

title: Home
text:
Pathways Flowchart
The Pathways Flowchart was developed as an alternative approach to the Openscapes Pathways Spreadsheet to reduce decision-related friction and identify priority projects for automation. This approach is more complicated to initiate than the spreadsheet, but it offers great flexibility and utility. With good documentation, this approach should facilitate adoption of streamlined open science methods while learning (and reinforcing) open science methods.
The goals of the Pathways Flowchart are to:

Create a visual representation of your workflow (Flowchart)
Evaluate the potential for streamlining/automation of each component
Prioritize components for improvement
Identify templates and examples to facilitate these improvements
Encourage a return to the Flowchart to revise and re-evaluate to identify the next priority for improvement
Encourage viewing this as an iterative process, improving one component at a time
Encourage sharing developments as part of the Open Science process
Provide alternative uses of these flowcharts to serve other data science needs

This work emphasizes the iteritive nature of this process, helping users identify workflow components in a flowchart, identifying their potential for automation, and prioritizing projects based on experience, need, and potential.



Workflow diagram for creating the Pathway Flowchart project. This project will create interactive diagrams that facilitate a visual representation of your workflow and the process of incremental adoption of an open science framework for each component of your workflow.

title: Dashboard Setup & Maintenance Guide
text:
This document covers everything you need to set up, populate, maintain, and extend the Examples Dashboard as a template repository owner or contributor.



your-repo/
│
├── dashboard.qmd                    ← Main dashboard page
├── instructions.qmd                 ← This guide
│
├── styles/
│   └── dashboard.css                ← All dashboard styling
│
├── content/
│   ├── examples.yaml                ← Single source of truth for all examples
│   └── card-images/                 ← Thumbnail/screenshot images
│       ├── placeholder.svg          ← Fallback shown when no image is set
│       └── my-example.png           ← One file per example (any format)
│
└── .github/
    ├── ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
    │   └── example-submission.yml   ← Structured submission form
    ├── workflows/
    │   └── process-submission.yml   ← GitHub Action (auto-PR on approval)
    └── scripts/
        └── parse_submission.py      ← Parses issue form → YAML entry






In dashboard.qmd, find this line in the R code chunk and replace YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO with your actual GitHub organisation and repository name:
submit_url <- paste0(
  "https://github.com/YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO",
  "/issues/new?template=example-submission.yml"
)



The dashboard uses the yaml package to read examples.yaml. Install it once:
install.packages("yaml")
Your renv.lock (if you use renv) should capture this automatically when you next run renv::snapshot().



The placeholder image is shown on cards where no image has been provided. A minimal SVG is included (content/card-images/placeholder.svg). You can replace it with your own branded placeholder — any common image format works (SVG, PNG, JPG).



A minimal Quarto website configuration:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

website:
  title: "Your Project Name"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: dashboard.qmd
        text: Examples
      - href: instructions.qmd
        text: Guide

format:
  html:
    theme: default



In your repository: Settings → Pages → Source → Deploy from branch → main → /docs.
After this, every push to main that includes a re-rendered docs/ folder will update the live site.







All examples live in content/examples.yaml. Each entry is one card on the dashboard. The file begins with a detailed comment header — read it before making edits.











Field
Required
Type
Notes




id
✅
integer
Unique. Always increment from the last entry.


title
✅
string
Short title for the card (~60 chars).


description
✅
string
1–3 sentences. Use the > block scalar for long text.


url
✅
string
Full public URL.


tools
✅
list
One or more tool categories (see below).


keywords
✅
list
One or more keywords (see below).


image
—
string
Filename only (e.g. my-example.png). File must be in content/card-images/. Leave blank "" for placeholder.


author
—
string
Person or organisation.


date_added
—
string
ISO date YYYY-MM-DD. For internal tracking.






- id: 7
  title: "My Open Science Workflow"
  description: >
    A reproducible end-to-end workflow for open science data analysis,
    from raw data ingestion through to publication-ready figures.
  url: "https://github.com/example/open-workflow"
  tools:
    - Reproducible Reports
    - Data Management
  keywords:
    - r
    - GitHub
    - template
  image: "open-workflow.png"
  author: "Jane Smith"
  date_added: "2025-03-01"



Unlike CSV, multi-value fields are just YAML lists — no need to wrap comma-separated values in quotes. Each tool or keyword is its own indented - item line.






Open content/examples.yaml in RStudio or any text editor.
Copy an existing entry block and paste it at the bottom of the examples: list.
Increment id by 1 from the previous last entry.
Fill in the fields.
If you have an image, save it in content/card-images/.
Render (quarto render) and verify the card appears correctly.
Commit and push.





Tools and keywords do not need to be registered anywhere — the dashboard derives its dropdown options automatically from all values present in examples.yaml at render time.
To add a new tool or keyword: use it in a new (or existing) entry. It will appear in the dropdown on the next render.
To remove a tool or keyword from the dropdowns: remove it from every entry that uses it.
To rename a tool or keyword: do a find-and-replace across examples.yaml.
The current default values are listed in the GitHub issue form template (example-submission.yml) — update that file too when you add new official tools or keywords so submitters see the full list.







1. Community member opens the GitHub issue using the "Submit a New
   Dashboard Example" template and fills in the structured form.

2. The issue is auto-labelled "example-submission" and "needs-review".

3. Admin reviews the issue content.

4. If approved, admin adds the "approved" label.
       ↓
   GitHub Action fires automatically:
     a. Parses the issue form body
     b. Appends a new entry to content/examples.yaml
     c. Opens a Pull Request on a branch named example/issue-N

5. Admin reviews the PR:
     a. Checks the new YAML entry looks correct
     b. Downloads any submitted image → saves to content/card-images/
     c. Verifies any new tool/keyword values are intentional
     d. Runs quarto preview locally (optional but recommended)

6. Admin merges the PR.

7. Admin re-renders and pushes docs/ (or CI does it automatically).

8. New card appears live on the dashboard. ✅




The workflow file (.github/workflows/process-submission.yml) is already in place. It needs two things before it will work:


Go to your repo → Issues → Labels → New label and create:








Label
Colour
Purpose




example-submission
any
Auto-applied by the issue template. Identifies submission issues.


needs-review
yellow
Auto-applied. Signals it’s awaiting admin review.


approved
green
Applied manually by admins to trigger the Action.


auto-generated
grey
Applied to PRs created by the Action.






Go to: Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions Select: Read and write permissions → Save.
This allows the Action to commit to the repository and open PRs using GITHUB_TOKEN (no additional secrets needed).






Open the new issue (labelled example-submission + needs-review).
Review: is the URL valid and publicly accessible? Is the description accurate? Are the tool/keyword selections appropriate?
If you want to reject it, close the issue with a comment explaining why.
If you approve it, add the approved label. The Action will run within ~1 minute.
Check the Actions tab to confirm the workflow completed successfully.
Find the new PR (it’s linked in a comment automatically posted to the issue).
In the PR, review content/examples.yaml in the diff.
If the submitter provided an image URL:

Download the image.
Rename it to match the image field in the new YAML entry.
Add it to content/card-images/ and commit it to the PR branch.

Merge the PR.
Re-render and push (quarto render → git add docs/ → git push).





If you prefer not to use the Action, or if something fails:

Read the issue carefully and extract the submitted values.
Open content/examples.yaml and manually append a new entry.
If an image was provided, download it to content/card-images/.
Commit, push, re-render.








# In your project directory (or use RStudio's Render button):
quarto render

# Live preview in browser with hot reload:
quarto preview



The standard workflow after any content update:
quarto render          # rebuilds docs/
git add docs/
git commit -m "render: update dashboard"
git push
GitHub Pages will serve the updated docs/ automatically within ~30 seconds.



Add a second workflow to render the site automatically on every push to main:
# .github/workflows/render-site.yml
name: Render and Deploy Site
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r@v2
        with:
          use-public-rspm: true

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies@v2
        with:
          packages: |
            any::tidyverse
            any::here
            any::yaml
            any::jsonlite

      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Render site
        run: quarto render

      - uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@v4
        with:
          folder: docs







All visual tokens are CSS custom properties at the top of styles/dashboard.css. Change these to retheme the entire dashboard:
:root {
  --ink:        #1a1410;   /* primary text */
  --ivory:      #f7f3ec;   /* page background */
  --terra:      #c4583a;   /* tool tag accent */
  --teal:       #2a6b6e;   /* keyword tag accent */
  --serif:      "Fraunces", Georgia, serif;
  --sans:       "DM Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
  --mono:       "DM Mono", monospace;
}
To swap fonts: update the Google Fonts <link> tag in the include-in-header section of dashboard.qmd, and update the --serif/--sans/--mono variables.



By default, selecting multiple tools uses OR logic: a card appears if it matches any selected tool. To require all selected tools to match (AND logic), edit the filtering OJS cell in dashboard.qmd:
// Change .some() to .every() for AND logic:
const toolMatch =
  selectedTools.length === 0 ||
  selectedTools.every(t => exTools.includes(t))   // ← every, not some

const kwMatch =
  selectedKeywords.length === 0 ||
  selectedKeywords.every(k => exKws.includes(k))  // ← every, not some




Add the new field to entries in examples.yaml.
In the R chunk in dashboard.qmd, add the field to the map_dfr() tibble.
In the OJS cards cell, reference ex.your_field inside renderCard().




When you add new official tools or keywords to examples.yaml, also add them to the checkboxes in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/example-submission.yml so that submitters can select them. The file is self-documented with comments.






Dashboard shows no examples / blank page

Confirm content/examples.yaml exists and is valid YAML (no tab characters — use spaces). Run yaml::read_yaml("content/examples.yaml") in R to check. Verify the here() path resolves correctly from your project root.

Images not appearing on cards

The filename in the image field must exactly match the file in content/card-images/ (case-sensitive on Linux/GitHub). The card silently falls back to the placeholder on load failure — open your browser’s DevTools console to see the actual 404 URL.

New tools/keywords not appearing in dropdowns

The dropdowns are built from the data at render time. Re-render after editing the YAML.

GitHub Action not firing on “approved” label

Check the label name is spelled exactly approved (case-sensitive). Check the Actions tab for errors. Confirm workflow permissions are set to “Read and write” in repository Settings.

parse_submission.py producing empty or wrong field values

GitHub renders issue form fields as ### Field Label\n\nvalue. If GitHub changes this format, the regex in the script may need updating. Print ISSUE_BODY in the Action’s logs to inspect the raw text, then adjust the extract_field() patterns accordingly.

renv doesn’t include the yaml package

Run install.packages("yaml") then renv::snapshot() to capture it.






If you improve this dashboard template and want to share changes upstream, please open an issue or PR in the original template repository. Suggestions for new default tools/keywords, design improvements, workflow enhancements, and accessibility fixes are all welcome.

text:
ReadMe for Supplement Folder
The purpose of this folder is to hold any supplementary files that are not data, script, or components of the manuscript.

title: Interactive Workflow Survey System - Setup Guide
text:
This system allows users to click on shapes in a workflow diagram and fill out surveys about each component. All responses are stored locally and can be downloaded as CSV files for version control in GitHub.



✅ Interactive Diagram - Pan, zoom, and click on shapes
✅ Sliding Survey Panel - Clean interface for data entry
✅ Password Protected - Simple shared password for editing
✅ Pre-filled Forms - Editing shows previous responses
✅ Complete History - All submissions are logged with timestamps
✅ CSV Export - Download data for GitHub commits
✅ Works Out of the Box - Minimal configuration needed







Copy these files to your project:

workflow-survey-system.qmd
add_survey_links.R (optional, for existing diagrams)

Place your SVG diagram in the output/ folder




Open workflow-survey-system.qmd and edit the R setup chunk:
# ============================================
# CONFIGURATION - EDIT THESE VALUES
# ============================================
SURVEY_PASSWORD <- "workflow2024"  # Change this to your desired password
DIAGRAM_FILE <- "OSworkflow.svg"   # Your SVG diagram filename
Change: - SURVEY_PASSWORD - Set your own password - DIAGRAM_FILE - Use your SVG filename



You have two options:



Open your diagram at https://app.diagrams.net/
Select a shape
Right-click → Edit Link (or Alt+Shift+L)
Enter link format: #survey:SHAPE_ID|SHAPE_TEXT

Example: #survey:data_collection|Data Collection
SHAPE_ID = Unique identifier (no spaces)
SHAPE_TEXT = Display name for the shape

Click Apply
Repeat for all shapes
File → Export as → SVG

✅ Check “Include a copy of my diagram”
✅ Check “Links”

Save to output/your_diagram.svg





Export your diagram from diagrams.net to SVG (without links)
Save it to output/ folder
In R Console:

source("add_survey_links.R")
add_survey_links_to_svg("output/your_diagram.svg")
This automatically adds survey links to all shapes!




In workflow-survey-system.qmd, find the survey form section and edit questions:
<div class="form-group">
  <label for="question1">Question 1: YOUR QUESTION HERE</label>
  <select id="question1" required>
    <option value="">-- Select --</option>
    <option value="Option1">Option 1</option>
    <option value="Option2">Option 2</option>
    <!-- Add more options -->
  </select>
</div>
To add a question: Copy a <div class="form-group"> block and: - Change question1 to question6, question7, etc. - Update the label text - Update the options
Don’t forget to also update: - The JavaScript saveResponse() function to include new questions - The CSV headers in downloadCSV() function




In RStudio, open workflow-survey-system.qmd
Click Render (or use quarto preview)
Click on a shape in your diagram
Fill out the survey
Enter password
Submit
Download CSV and commit to GitHub









View Diagram - Navigate to the page with your workflow
Click a Shape - Survey panel slides out from the right
Review Previous Entry - If you’ve submitted before, see your last response
Edit/Update - Form is pre-filled, make changes as needed
Submit - Enter password and submit
Download Data - Scroll to bottom, click “Download All Responses (CSV)”
Commit to GitHub - Save the CSV file and push to your repo




When users first clone your template:

They get a working survey system out of the box
They only need to:

Change the password in the config
Add their own diagram
Optionally customize questions







your-project/
├── workflow-survey-system.qmd    # Main Quarto document
├── add_survey_links.R            # Helper function (optional)
├── output/
│   └── your_diagram.svg          # Your workflow diagram
├── _site/                        # Rendered output (auto-generated)
│   └── workflow-survey-system.html
└── data/                         # Store downloaded CSVs here
    └── workflow_responses_YYYY-MM-DD.csv




Links in your SVG should follow this format:
#survey:SHAPE_ID|SHAPE_TEXT
Examples:

#survey:step1|Data Collection
#survey:analysis_phase|Analysis Phase
#survey:qa_review|QA Review

Rules: - SHAPE_ID must be unique for each shape - No spaces in SHAPE_ID (use underscores) - SHAPE_TEXT can have spaces and special characters - Separate ID and text with | (pipe character)







LocalStorage: Responses are saved in your browser’s localStorage
Per User: Each person who opens the page on their computer has their own data
Persistent: Data stays even after closing the browser
Exportable: Download as CSV anytime

Can analyze responses with R scripts

const response = {
  timestamp: new Date().toISOString(),
  username: document.getElementById('username').value,
  shape_id: currentShapeId,
  shape_text: currentShapeText,

Edit the R setup chunk:

Replace the SVG file and update:

Check link format: Must be #survey:ID|TEXT
Check SVG export: “Links” option must be checked
Check browser console: Press F12, look for errors





Check you haven’t changed the password in the qmd but not re-rendered
Password is case-sensitive
Make sure you’re using the same password in config and when submitting





This is normal if it’s the first time clicking that shape
Check browser localStorage (F12 → Application → Local Storage)
Make sure you submitted with the correct password





Check if you have responses (look at count at bottom)
Try a different browser
Check browser’s download settings





LocalStorage should persist, but:
Make sure you’re using the same browser
Check if browser is in private/incognito mode (data won’t persist)
Solution: Download CSV regularly!








Once you’ve downloaded CSVs, you can analyze them:
library(tidyverse)

# Read all response files
responses <- list.files("data", pattern = "workflow_responses.*\\.csv", 
                       full.names = TRUE) %>%
  map_df(read_csv)

# View summary by shape
responses %>%
  group_by(shape_id, shape_text) %>%
  summarize(
    n_responses = n(),
    latest_status = last(question1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Timeline of updates
responses %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(timestamp)) %>%
  count(date, shape_id) %>%
  ggplot(aes(date, n, color = shape_id)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(title = "Survey Responses Over Time")



To have multiple diagrams with surveys:

Create separate .qmd files for each diagram
Each can have its own password and questions
Responses are stored separately per page (based on URL)




Since localStorage is per-browser:

Team members download their CSVs regularly
Commit to a shared GitHub repo
Merge CSV files in R for team analysis






Q: Can multiple people edit the same shape?
A: Yes! Each person’s submissions are logged separately with timestamps and usernames.
Q: What if I forget the password?
A: Change it in the R config chunk and re-render the page.
Q: Can I delete a response?
A: Not directly in the UI, but you can download the CSV, remove the row, and that’s your new record.
Q: Is this secure?
A: This is designed for internal team use with a shared password. It’s not meant for sensitive data requiring encryption.
Q: Can I use this without GitHub?
A: Yes! The survey system works locally. You just need to manually share CSV files.
Q: How many responses can it handle?
A: LocalStorage typically allows 5-10MB. At ~200 bytes per response, that’s 25,000-50,000 responses. More than enough!




For issues or questions: 1. Check this documentation 2. Review the code comments 3. Open an issue on GitHub 4. Contact the template creator




This template is open source and free to use for your projects!

title: Interactive Workflow Survey System - Setup Guide
text: This system allows users to click on shapes in a workflow diagram and fill out surveys about each component. All responses are stored locally and can be downloaded as CSV files for version control in GitHub.

text:

Copy these files to your project:

Open workflow-survey-system.qmd and edit the R setup chunk:

SURVEY_PASSWORD <- "workflow2024"  # Change this to your desired password
DIAGRAM_FILE <- "OSworkflow.svg"   # Your SVG diagram filename
Change: - SURVEY_PASSWORD - Set your own password - DIAGRAM_FILE - Use your SVG filename



You have two options:



Open your diagram at https://app.diagrams.net/
Select a shape
Right-click → Edit Link (or Alt+Shift+L)
Enter link format: #survey:SHAPE_ID|SHAPE_TEXT

SHAPE_ID = Unique identifier (no spaces)
SHAPE_TEXT = Display name for the shape

Click Apply
Repeat for all shapes

✅ Check “Include a copy of my diagram”
✅ Check “Links”

Save to output/your_diagram.svg





Export your diagram from diagrams.net to SVG (without links)
Save it to output/ folder
In R Console:

source("add_survey_links.R")
add_survey_links_to_svg("output/your_diagram.svg")
This automatically adds survey links to all shapes!




In workflow-survey-system.qmd, find the survey form section and edit questions:
<div class="form-group">

    <option value="">-- Select --</option>

    <!-- Add more options -->
  </select>
</div>
To add a question: Copy a <div class="form-group"> block and: - Change question1 to question6, question7, etc. - Update the label text - Update the options
Don’t forget to also update: - The JavaScript saveResponse() function to include new questions - The CSV headers in downloadCSV() function

Fill out the survey
Enter password
Submit
Download CSV and commit to GitHub

Not: step_1, step_2, ..., step_9, step_10

R function to automatically convert Word document headings into multi-page Draw.io flowcharts.



✅ Converts .docx headings to Draw.io flowchart diagrams
✅ Sequential H1 spine: H1 headings connected in order
✅ H2 branches: H2s branch perpendicular from their parent H1
✅ Smart layout: TB direction = H1 vertical, H2 horizontal right; LR direction = H1 horizontal, H2 vertical down
✅ Color-coded rounded rectangles by heading hierarchy
✅ Multi-page support: Each H2 gets its own sub-page
✅ Link buttons: H2s on overview are clickable links (marked with 🔗) to sub-pages
✅ Configurable flow direction (top-to-bottom or left-to-right)
✅ Exclude specific heading levels
✅ Automatic “Back to Overview” links on sub-pages
✅ Tidyverse-compatible syntax with %>% pipes




install.packages(c("officer", "xml2", "dplyr", "purrr"))



# Source the function
source("docx_to_drawio.R")

# Create flowchart from your Word document
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "output",
  direction = "TB",
  h2_new_page = TRUE
)














Parameter
Type
Default
Description




input_file
character
required
Path to .docx file


output_folder
character
required
Folder where .drawio file will be saved


exclude_levels
numeric vector
NULL
Heading levels to exclude (e.g., c(3, 4))


direction
character
"TB"
Flow direction: "TB" (top-bottom) or "LR" (left-right)


h2_new_page
logical
TRUE
If TRUE, each H2 creates a new diagram page


output_filename
character
NULL
Custom filename without extension (auto-generated if NULL)







The function uses a default color palette for heading hierarchy:

H1: Blue (#4A90E2)
H2: Medium Purple (#7B68EE)
H3: Emerald (#50C878)
H4: Sandy Brown (#F4A460)
H5: Light Coral (#E57373)
H6: Gold (#FFD700)






H1-1 → H2a → H2b
 ↓
H1-2
 ↓
H1-3 → H2c → H2d → H2e

H1 spine: Vertical (top-to-bottom)
H2 branches: Horizontal to the right
Connections: H1s connected sequentially; H2s under same H1 connected horizontally




H1-1 → H1-2 → H1-3
 ↓              ↓
H2a            H2c
 ↓              ↓
H2b            H2d
               ↓
              H2e

H1 spine: Horizontal (left-to-right)
H2 branches: Vertical downward
Connections: H1s connected sequentially; H2s under same H1 connected vertically





When h2_new_page = TRUE:
Overview Page: - Contains all H1 and H2 headings - H1 boxes show the main document structure (sequential spine) - H2 boxes are clickable link buttons (marked with 🔗 icon and dashed borders) - Clicking an H2 navigates to its dedicated sub-page
Sub-Pages (one per H2): - Contains the H2 heading + all its children (H3, H4, etc.) - H2 box appears here as an editable node (this is the “real” version) - Shows hierarchical tree structure of that section - Includes “Back to Overview” button to return to main page
Note: H2 boxes appear on overview as navigation links only. Edit the actual H2 content on its sub-page.





library(officer)
source("docx_to_drawio.R")

# Convert document with default settings
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "flowcharts"
)



# Create horizontal flowchart
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "flowcharts",
  direction = "LR"
)



# Exclude H3 and H4 headings from diagram
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "flowcharts",
  exclude_levels = c(3, 4)
)



# Put all headings on one page (no H2 sub-pages)
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "flowcharts",
  h2_new_page = FALSE
)



# Specify custom output filename
create_flowchart(
  input_file = "my_document.docx",
  output_folder = "flowcharts",
  output_filename = "project_workflow_v2"
)



library(dplyr)

# Use in a pipeline
"my_document.docx" %>%
  create_flowchart(
    output_folder = "flowcharts",
    exclude_levels = 4,
    direction = "TB"
  )



# Process multiple documents
library(purrr)

docx_files <- list.files("documents", pattern = "\\.docx$", full.names = TRUE)

docx_files %>%
  walk(~create_flowchart(
    input_file = .x,
    output_folder = "flowcharts",
    direction = "TB"
  ))




Your Word document should use built-in heading styles:
Heading 1 (H1)
├── Heading 2 (H2)
│   ├── Heading 3 (H3)
│   └── Heading 3 (H3)
├── Heading 2 (H2)
│   ├── Heading 3 (H3)
│   └── Heading 3 (H3)
└── Heading 2 (H2)

Heading 1 (H1)
└── Heading 2 (H2)
    └── Heading 3 (H3)
Important: Use Word’s built-in heading styles (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.), not manually formatted text.



The function creates a .drawio XML file that can be:

Opened in Draw.io:

Go to https://app.diagrams.net
File → Open → Select your .drawio file

Imported into other tools: Compatible with diagrams.net integrations
Edited: Full Draw.io functionality available for customization




your-project/
├── docx_to_drawio.R         # Main function file
├── example_usage.R           # Example scripts
├── your_document.docx        # Input Word document
└── output/                   # Output folder
    └── your_document_flowchart.drawio





Problem: “No headings found in document” error
Solution: Ensure your document uses Word’s built-in Heading styles (not just bold text)



Problem: Some headings don’t appear in flowchart
Solution: Check if they’re excluded via exclude_levels parameter



Problem: Boxes overlap or spacing looks off
Solution: Try changing direction parameter or use single-page mode



Problem: Output file not created
Solution: Ensure output_folder path exists or can be created




To modify colors, spacing, or node sizes, edit these internal variables in docx_to_drawio.R:
# In get_heading_color() function - change colors
colors <- c(
  "1" = "#4A90E2",  # Your custom H1 color
  "2" = "#7B68EE",  # Your custom H2 color
  # ...
)

# In calculate_layout() function - change spacing
node_width <- 180      # Box width
node_height <- 60      # Box height
h_spacing <- 80        # Horizontal spacing
v_spacing <- 100       # Vertical spacing




The function preserves heading hierarchy from your Word document
Empty headings are included but may appear as empty boxes
Very large documents may create complex diagrams - consider excluding lower heading levels
Draw.io page links work when opened in draw.io/diagrams.net




Free to use and modify for your projects.



For issues or questions, refer to the example scripts in example_usage.R.

text:
ReadMe for Manuscript Folder
The purpose of this folder is to provide a home to one or more versions of the manuscript in one or more formats. This folder does not serve as the output location for rendered versions of the manuscript (those would be saved in the docs folder). Typically, this would be reserved for final manuscripts or manuscripts saved in an alternative format or manually modified (not direct rendered output).
Note: to create a template with standardized folder structure, there needs to be a document within the folder to save to github. This readme serves as this document, and can be deleted from your personal project/repository.

title: Interactive Workflow Tool
text:
Instructions for Users

How to Use This System

Click on any shape in the diagram to open the survey panel
Fill out the survey with your observations
Enter the password (ask your administrator)
Submit - your response is saved locally in your browser
Download CSV regularly and commit to your GitHub repository
Return later - the form will pre-fill with your previous answers



For Template Users
When you copy this template: 1. Change the SURVEY_PASSWORD in the R setup chunk 2. Replace DIAGRAM_FILE with your SVG filename 3. Add survey links to your shapes (see QUICK_REFERENCE.md) 4. Customize the survey questions as needed

text:

Now that your diagram is ready and saved to your ‘output’ folder, you need to modify the tool so it can find your diagram.

Open your project in Rstudio
Open the ‘flowchart-pathways.qmd’ in the content/ folder

title: Working with Workflows
text:
For many, creating a diagram of your workflow is an intuitive approach to identifying the parts of the process, and from there we can start to think about how to transition those parts into an open source framework. If this approach feels right for you, then this template serves to simplify this adoption in an open source way.
Sure, you can sketch your workflow on a piece of paper, but by following this process, you will learn skills, and the diagrams you develop can be integrated into your developing open source framework. Ultimately, do what is best for you, but know this template is here for you to follow.




Workflow diagram for creating the Pathway Flowchart project. This project will create interactive diagrams that facilitate a visual representation of your workflow and the process of incremental adoption of an open science framework for each component of your workflow.

title: Working with Workflows
section: What do I need to Start?
text:
What do I need to Start?
You will need to work in R/Quarto (RStudio or Positron) and Github. We also recommend Github Desktop to facilitate working locally and saving to the cloud (github). If you are new, we recommend the PSAW 2022 R/Github Training Sessions to get started.

title: Working with Workflows
text:
Then what?

Getting Started: Create your own Pathways Repo by saving this template to your own Github Repo
Flowchart Build: Build a Flowchart using your existing documentation and/or the draw.io web application
Flowchart Refine: Refine your flowchart and prepare for Pathways Flowchart
Pathways Flowchart: Compile surveys for your Pathways Flowchart
Decision Tool: The decision tool will evaluate your pathways survey responses to identify workflow components for prioritization.
Open Science Dashboard: Use keywords to find a template to use or an example to fork. Cheating is Encouraged!
Get it Done: Work on your project! This is independent of the Pathways Flowchart, but we encourage you to find a community to help you tackle your goals.
Giving Back: If your project can serve as a good example or if it has new ways of solving a problem, we encourage you to share it as an example for others to consider!
Revise and Revisit: Revise and Revisit your workflow, and move onto the next project!

Follow the button on the bottom right to get started (or follow the process using the left sidebar)

title: workflow-givingBack
text: Share your examples and templates for others to access using the Example Dashboard

title: Build your Flowchart
text:
We have two methods for building your first draft flowchart:
The automated approach is useful for complex diagrams where there is documentation (or an outline) of all components with Headers in H1, H2, and H3 format (see below). For simple diagrams, it may be easier to build directly in draw.io (see next page).

title: Build your Flowchart
text:
Automate Diagram Build from Text
This function will allow you to build a draft draw.io workflow diagram from an existing text document (docx) with headers:

Level 1 Headers (H1) will serve as the primary workflow
Level 2 headers will branch off from these (these will include lower level headers can be opened in a new draw.io page for clarity).
Once this draft flowchart is created, go to the next page to ‘Refine your Flowchart’







NoteNote:



For more information, see the docx_to_drawio_README in the _code folder.


Instructions:

Test with Sample Data Plan

Open your RStudio project for this repository.
Open flowchart-doc2draw.qmd (in the content/ folder). Alternatively, you can copy this code, below, into your own R file)
Run the code chunk as-is to build a draft diagram using the SampleDataPlan.
You can follow the instructions on the next page (Refine your Flowchart) to open/view it, if you like, or you can move on to create your custom flowchart.

Custom docx –> Flowchart

Add your docx file to the ‘output’ folder. Double-check that all headings are at the level you want need for this project (this function expects H1 to be the top level, and then H2, etc.).
Modify the path for ‘path_to_docx’ to select your personal docx file.
Run the code to create your own flowchart (name will be based on the name of your original docx).
Open in draw.io to refine (see next page, Refine your Flowchart).



library(here)
source(here::here("_code/docx_to_drawio.R"))
path_to_docx <- here("output/SampleDataPlan.docx")
path_to_output <- here("output/")

# Create Flowchart in draw.io based on your docx headers

  create_flowchart(
    input_file = path_to_docx,
    output_folder = path_to_output,
    direction = "TB",
    h2_new_page = TRUE
  )

Alternatively, you can build your diagram using draw.io

title: Interactive Workflow Tool
text:
Practice with this example flowchart to see how this works:

Use your mouse (or the buttoms) to zoom in, zoom out, then zoom in again
Use your mouse to ‘grab’ and drag the zoomed in flowchart to move the zoomed in view by clicking and holding on the image while moving your mouse.
Select the Flowchart Component that says “Flow Charts” (move your mouse to hover over this box until you can click to select it). A survey should open. Add your name and for the password, put “workflow2026”.

This flowchart shows the workflow for using the Pathways Flowchart tool– so you can practice with this example while you are learning to use the tool. In this case, you can submit responses truthfully as it relates to your experience with the tool. Or not.
When you are done, submit the response.
When you have finished all responses, scroll down and choose ‘Download All Responses’. This will save responses to a csv file in your repository. As you improve components of your workflow, you can revisit these surveys and update the reponses (which will update the csv). This information is used in the Decision Tool, so updated responses are helpful!

text:
Practice with this example flowchart to see how this works:

Use your mouse (or the buttoms) to zoom in, zoom out, then zoom in again
Use your mouse to ‘grab’ and drag the zoomed in flowchart to move the zoomed in view by clicking and holding on the image while moving your mouse.
Select the Flowchart Component that says “Flow Charts” (move your mouse to hover over this box until you can click to select it). A survey should open. Add your name and for the password, put “workflow2026”.

This flowchart shows the workflow for using the Pathways Flowchart tool– so you can practice with this example while you are learning to use the tool. In this case, you can submit responses truthfully as it relates to your experience with the tool. Or not.
When you are done, submit the response.
When you have finished all responses, scroll down and choose ‘Download All Responses’. This will save responses to a csv file in your repository. As you improve components of your workflow, you can revisit these surveys and update the reponses (which will update the csv). This information is used in the Decision Tool, so updated responses are helpful!

text:
Instructions for Users

How to Use This System

Click on any shape in the diagram to open the survey panel
Fill out the survey with your observations
Enter the password (ask your administrator)
Submit - your response is saved locally in your browser
Download CSV regularly and commit to your GitHub repository
Return later - the form will pre-fill with your previous answers



For Template Users
When you copy this template: 1. Change the SURVEY_PASSWORD in the R setup chunk 2. Replace DIAGRAM_FILE with your SVG filename 3. Add survey links to your shapes (see QUICK_REFERENCE.md) 4. Customize the survey questions as needed

title: Getting Started
text:
This template will create a Github Repository standard folders and embedded flowcharts and the foundation for a Quarto website that can serve as a tool for navigating your Openscapes Pathways, a Lab Manual with embedded flowcharts, or as an experimental repository.
Follow the steps below to copy this template to your lab’s GitHub

Open [this repository on GitHub]
Click on the ‘Use this template’ button in the top right, select ‘Create new repository’

Edit repository settings

Select the ‘Include all branches’ option
Select your lab organization or yourself as the owner and set the repository name to your Project Name
Make description “Research Compendium for Project Name”
Make the repo public


Click create repository
Turn on GitHub pages under Settings->Pages. You will set pages to be made from the gh-pages branch and root directory

Update ‘Actions’ settings

Under Settings -> Actions -> General -> Actions permissions select ‘Allow all actions and reusable workflows’. Select save

Under Settings -> Actions -> General -> Workflow permissions select ‘Read and write permissions’







ImportantNote: Both of these settings may already be selected but you have to click save for both







Return to the main page of the repo to edit your website

Click on ‘Code’ in the top left
Click on the settings button next to the ‘About’ section on the right side of the repository
Select ‘Use your GitHub Pages website’. This should autopopulate the website
Select ‘Save Changes’


The foundation for your project is now created! We recommend that you first modify the ReadMe to add your Project Name in the appropriate places.
